Decision: Going full-time and entering Horizon 2 are explicitly NOT the same event
Rationale: There is a window of full-time-but-still-Horizon-1 where the founder works full-time on the existing wedge before earning the right to expand scope. Prevents failure mode of 'I went full-time, so I should be doing more things.' Reinforces earned-evolution pillar.

Decision: Strike 'non-profit arm' from Strategy Doc; replace with mission-aligned pricing tier
Rationale: Original 'non-profit arm' language implied a structural commitment (separate 501(c)(3) entity) that founder did not actually mean. New language: 'Mission-aligned pricing tier within the for-profit company for under-resourced institutions, education, cultural. Deferred to Horizon 3+. Exact structure and qualification criteria TBD.'

Decision: First co-founder/senior hire post-MVP: role decision deferred
Rationale: Will be made when proof signals hit, based on which gap is most painful at that moment. Candidate roles are creative director/design lead, technical co-founder, or GTM co-founder. Avoids pre-committing to a hire profile before knowing which gap is actually blocking. Watchpoint: deliberate evaluation must happen before talking to candidates, not while talking to them.

Decision: Adopted one-line description of the company
Rationale: Selected variant emphasizing developer/technical positioning, then revised to use 'adaptive experiences' instead of 'narrative personalization' (founder felt latter was diminishing). Final form: '[Company] is building the infrastructure for adaptive experiences: an API for stories, games, and simulations that respond to the people inside them, and the games that prove what becomes possible.' Colon structure preserves rhythm without em dashes.

Decision: Year range guidance: H1 = 6-12mo, H2 = 12-24mo, H3+ = 24+ mo
Rationale: Tighter than original 12-18/12-30/30+ proposal. Founder chose ambitious-stretch interpretation. Watchpoint: 6-month floor is aggressive while building part-time; 12-month floor is more realistic. Treat 6-month as ambitious-stretch goal, not commitment.

Decision: Proof signal framework adopted: 2-of-3 product AND 2-of-3 business signals to go full-time
Rationale: Same base criteria for Horizon 1-to-2 transition, plus Horizon 2-specific signals (TBD). Two-axis test ensures both product and business validation before scope expansion or full-time commitment.

Decision: Source of truth for documents: Notion (database-structured workspace)
Rationale: Notion chosen over Google Drive for database structure (Decisions Log, Open Questions, future trackers), cross-linking between artifacts, and queryability via connector. Foundation artifacts created directly in Notion via connector rather than manual paste-from-markdown workflow.

Decision: Revenue-based financing accepted as Stage 1 alternative if terms preserve control
Rationale: Founder explicitly opted in. Provides non-dilutive path for founders willing to accept structured payback. Same control-preserving constraints as equity.

Decision: Founder control operationally defined
Rationale: Founder-controlled means: 51%+ voting control indefinitely, 51%+ economic ownership through capital-efficient growth as long as feasible, no unilateral investor power to redirect mission/force exit/remove founder/override strategy without founder consent, board seats earned by contribution not granted as permanent authority. Provides operational definition that capital strategy can reference when evaluating term sheets.

Decision: Horizon 2 enterprise wedge: team building and corporate events (combined as one category)
Rationale: Confirmed as the right wedge. Combined to capture related buyer profiles.

Decision: Decline for now: clinical healthcare, K-12 institutional sales, therapy as clinical product
Rationale: Regulatory burden, sales-cycle pain, and clinical risk are too high for current company size. Revisit only with strategic partners who handle regulation.

Decision: Horizon 1 hiring rule: case-by-case, no hard contractor-only mandate
Rationale: Default leans toward contractors. Equity-bearing collaborators are an exception requiring deliberate written justification, vesting schedule (4-year with 1-year cliff standard), and reverse-vesting clause. Looser than original 'contractors only' rule, but with guardrails to prevent casual equity grants in early stage.

Decision: Adopted six strategic pillars
Rationale: Pillars: (1) Earned-evolution; (2) Founder-controlled and profit-first; (3) Platform and games both get protected time; (4) Human-led, AI-augmented; (5) Quality and ambition over scale and speed; (6) Build for unrepeatability. Original draft had seven candidates; pillars 4 and 5 (human-led + co-authorship) collapsed into one. Renamed 'Pixar-first, Valve-second, Stripe-later' to 'earned-evolution' for less informal phrasing.

Decision: Capital staging defined: Stage 0 bootstrap, Stage 1 $100K-$500K @ 5-10%, Stage 2 $1M-$3M @ 15-20%, Stage 3+ Series A only if scaling on founder's terms
Rationale: Profit-first capital efficiency. Each stage is optional and triggered by specific conditions, not a default fundraising path. Default assumption: no Series A unless something specific changes the calculus.

Decision: Reframe Strategy Doc framing from 'platform plus games' to 'creative work plus the infrastructure that makes ambitious creative work possible'
Rationale: Mission-discovery surfaced that creative work is the spine, infrastructure is the means. Original platform-first framing misread the founder's center of gravity. Pixar-first, Valve-second, Stripe-later progression.

Decision: Adopted mission statement (v1)
Rationale: Two-sentence form: 'Our mission is to make adaptive experiences that move people. We do it by treating every player as a co-author, not an audience, so that imagination is the only ceiling.' Two sentences chosen for separation of what (sentence 1) from how (sentence 2). Built via fill-in-the-blank scaffolding from founder's responses to mission-discovery questions.

Decision: Horizon 1 narrowed to a single wedge vertical: adult social party games, with Nightcap as the wedge product
Rationale: Original list had four active verticals (games, interactive fiction, kids', team building). Four verticals at once is too dilutive for a part-time founder. Other verticals move to Horizon 2 or later.

Decision: Three-horizon framing uses proof-signal-gated phases
Rationale: Year ranges are illustrative guidance only, not commitments. Avoids brittleness of calendar-based commitments while building part-time. Phases advance based on what's been proven, not what date it is.

Decision: Platform name committed: Arcwright
Rationale: Arcwright selected as platform name after a two-round candidate process. Compound coinword: arc (narrative arc, structural span, precision) plus wright (craftsperson, as in playwright, wheelwright). Cleared all eight due-diligence checks: no trademark conflicts found, http://arcwright.com/.ai/.io appear unregistered, social handles available, no negative linguistic associations, no active brand conflicts in adjacent spaces. Pretentiousness flag (wright suffix not universally known) raised and consciously accepted. Finalists considered before Arcwright was selected: Narrata (eliminated: http://narrat.dev active in same space), Lore (eliminated: generic in gaming, not trademarkable), PlotForge (eliminated: three active competitors using exact name). See 04-Naming-and-Brand-v1 for full due diligence record.

Decision: Game name (murder mystery): Nightcap retained pending trademark counsel clearance; fallback committed as Revel
Rationale: Nightcap retained as working title pending formal trademark counsel review. Two material conflicts identified: Studio Nightcap (active indie game studio in Pittsburgh, currently developing a murder mystery game, same genre as Nightcap) and NBA Nightcap (Amazon Prime Video post-game show, active Class 41 entertainment brand). Counsel must clear or reject before any public launch materials use the name. Fallback committed: Revel. Revel selected for warmth, social energy, and clean conflict landscape. Last Call ruled out (active murder mystery party game product). Guilty Party ruled out (Disney Interactive 2011 Wii murder mystery game, same genre, uncertain trademark lapse status). Monster RPG name deferred to Chat 8 (Story Bible: Monster RPG).

Decision: Company name committed: Arcwright Studios publicly; Arcwright Studios Inc. as legal entity on incorporation
Rationale: Company name unified with platform name. Studios suffix leans into Horizon 1 creative identity (Pixar-shape: creative studio with internal infrastructure). Horizon 2 friction (selling a B2B API platform under a Studios name) explicitly named and consciously accepted. Legal entity: Arcwright Studios Inc., to be filed in Delaware on incorporation. No conflicts found for Arcwright Studios as a compound: Arc Games, Arc System Works, and arc GAME STUDIO are distinct names in different territory.

Decision: Individual AI roleplay training is explicitly declined as an Arcwright market category
Rationale: This category (sales roleplay, skills training tools) is crowded and requires deep LMS/CRM integration that is incompatible with Arcwright's current stage and platform direction. Adaptive individual learning (e.g., DuoLingo-style personalization) is a separate, future vertical signal that may be on-mission post-MVP but is not the same category and is not a current priority.

Decision: A Product Requirements Document (PRD) session is required before Chat 6 (Technical Architecture)
Rationale: Without a PRD, Chat 6 becomes half requirements-gathering and half architecture, which wastes the architecture session. A lightweight PRD (1-2 pages capturing what the Arcwright engine must do, key design constraints, non-negotiables, integration surfaces) gives Chat 6 clear requirements to design against.

Decision: Enterprise Horizon 2 wedge is team building and corporate events (combined), explicitly not individual corporate training
Rationale: Competitive analysis confirmed the individual AI roleplay training market (Second Nature, Mindtickle, Hyperbound, etc.) is crowded, commoditizing, and requires deep LMS/CRM integration from day one. It is the wrong wedge. The enterprise category of AI-powered group narrative experience for team building does not yet exist as a recognized product category. No established competitor is selling it. This is the open territory for Horizon 2.

Decision: The Arcwright platform moat is personalization depth, not authoring tooling
Rationale: Authoring tooling can be commoditized. The depth of runtime personalization (how well the platform understands who is in the room, their history, preferences, and relationships) is the differentiator that determines the ceiling of what any designed arc can produce. This is an architectural priority, not a product features priority.

Decision: Content safety architecture: layered model with platform hard stops and developer-configurable rails
Rationale: Platform enforces absolute hard stops (CSAM, real violence instructions) at the model call layer, not the output filter layer. Everything outside hard stops is developer-configurable. The murder mystery genre is adjacent to dark content. The Latitude/AI Dungeon CSAM crisis is the failure mode this architecture exists to prevent.

Decision: Developer offering at MVP: API plus hosted dashboard
Rationale: Developers get an API (session management, character management, knowledge state management, content event streaming) and a hosted dashboard (arc authoring, session monitoring, usage management). No-code arc builder for non-developer creators is explicitly deferred post-MVP. Dashboard assumes developers who can read structured data.

Decision: North star progression confirmed: Pixar to Valve to Stripe (B to A)"
Rationale: Arcwright starts as a creative studio with internal infrastructure (Pixar-shape), grows to studio-plus-platform with a small external developer base (Valve-shape), and matures into a platform-first company with an active studio (Stripe-shape). Games are the primary product and identity through at least H1 and H2. The platform API opens to external developers only after internal games have proven it. This progression was implicit in the strategy document; this session made it explicit as a filter for all product decisions.

Decision: PRD scope set to full professional document (up to 10 pages)
Rationale: The Decisions Log from Chat 5 described a lightweight PRD (1-2 pages). The Chat 5.5 prompt specified a full professional PRD. Founder confirmed full scope: buildable specification for a technical architect, PM, and future co-founder, not a constraints brief.

Decision: Unified character model with split behavior source
Rationale: Every character (human-controlled or AI-driven) is the same object type: identity, personality profile, goals, knowledge state, relationship graph. Behavior source differs: AI characters have a generation engine; human characters have an input channel. Required for the monster RPG (player and NPC are interchangeable) and for Nightcap (players are characters in the story, not observers).

Decision: Multi-surface routing follows the Jackbox model: platform emits tagged content events, game layer handles routing
Rationale: The platform produces content events tagged with context (who the event is for, what triggered it, current session state). The game layer (Nightcap) decides what renders on which surface. The platform never knows what a TV or phone is. Keeps the engine surface-agnostic and generalizable to any delivery format.

Decision: Knowledge state management is always included, never optional; schema complexity is what is tiered by plan
Rationale: Making knowledge state optional would allow developers to ship experiences where AI characters reveal information they should not know, producing incoherent sessions that damage the platform reputation. The platform always enforces knowledge boundaries; the developer defines the schema. Complexity of schema determines cost tier. Competitor failures at this problem (Inworld, Convai) are the evidence base.

Decision: Cost model: value-based subscription with included AI action credits, internal model routing, premium workflow multipliers, usage dashboards, and overage/upgrade paths
Rationale: Users buy predictable access to outcomes; Arcwright manages AI cost complexity behind the routing layer. Hybrid model outperforms fixed-per-session (fragile at variable session cost) and raw token-based pricing (poor UX for non-developers). Internal model routing is a margin lever. Heavy usage pays more via overage or tier upgrade.

Decision: Nightcap MVP session: 4-10 players, flexible duration, one murder arc, AI-assigned killer, structured reveal sequence
Rationale: Player range of 4-10 accommodates typical party sizes. Flexible duration avoids hard clock constraints requiring additional pacing design. AI-assigned killer makes every session unique without host configuration. Structured reveal sequence ensures the arc ends with narrative coherence regardless of how well players solved the mystery.

Decision: Session interruption handling: partial persistence (character and knowledge state saved, arc restarts from nearest designed beat)
Rationale: On interruption, platform saves character state and knowledge state. On resume, arc restarts from the nearest designed beat. Sessions never restart from the beginning; no state is permanently lost. Full mid-scene resume is deferred as scope debt; beat-level resume covers the most common interruption scenarios and is sufficient for MVP.

Decision: Artifact numbering conflict flagged: PRD created as 06, but Technical Architecture was originally numbered 06
Rationale: 06-PRD-v1 was created in Chat 5.5. The original numbering scheme in 00-Project-Operating-Instructions listed 06 as Technical Architecture. Recommended resolution: renumber Technical Architecture as 07 and shift all subsequent artifacts up by one. Requires founder to update 00-Project-Operating-Instructions and 01-Strategy-Document Section 9 before Chat 6 begins.

Decision: Nightcap v1 success criteria: qualitative bar is the gate, quantitative metrics are the instrument
Rationale: Qualitative gate: five or more completed sessions with real groups outside the founder's immediate circle expressing unprompted enthusiasm. Quantitative instruments: session completion rate, arc pacing variance, killer concealment rate, replay intent. Metrics diagnose what to iterate on; they do not define shipped. Aligns with proof signal framework in 01-Strategy-Document.

Decision: Progressive proprietary infrastructure: three-tier build sequence (deterministic now, fine-tuned models at scale, foundation models never)
Rationale: Tier 1 at MVP: deterministic infrastructure with no AI cost (arc engine, session state, knowledge graph, routing, API). Tier 2 at Horizon 2 earliest, triggered by volume and cost data: fine-tuned open source models. Tier 3, never: foundation model development or compute ownership. Games are the data flywheel; session telemetry from day one is the mechanism. Competitors using the same APIs cannot replicate Arcwright-specific session data. Premature Tier 2 investment before data and economics justify it is a distraction from shipping.

Decision: Session telemetry is a named MVP done-criterion; must be live from the first production session
Rationale: Sessions without structured telemetry are compute cost with no data return. The Tier 2 fine-tuning transition is impossible without accumulated labeled session data. Minimum signals from first production session: arc beat engagement duration, pacing intervention triggers and outcomes, knowledge state constraint activations, session completion status, replay intent indicators. Richer schema designed in Chat 6.

Decision: Provider-agnostic model routing is a named architectural requirement, not an implication
Rationale: No platform operation may hard-code a dependency on any specific AI provider or model. All model calls route through an internal abstraction layer. Enables cost optimization through provider switching, compliance flexibility for enterprise customers, and Tier 2 migration from managed APIs to fine-tuned open source models without changing arc execution logic.

Decision: Games are deliberate platform stress-tests; each game's design brief must explicitly name which platform capabilities it validates
Rationale: The platform roadmap is sequenced around validation targets, not just creative preference. Nightcap validates linear arc execution, per-player knowledge state, multi-character ensemble, multi-surface routing, dynamic pacing. Monster RPG validates world state persistence, emergent narrative, procedural generation, player-defined motivation inference. Third game selection evaluated against remaining capability gaps. This discipline distinguishes platform-by-design from platform-by-accident.

Decision: Account model: hybrid (mandatory host accounts plus anonymous player join plus optional post-session account creation plus decoupled consent)
Rationale: Players join anonymously via QR code (preserves PRD frictionless onboarding requirement, under 30 seconds to seated). Hosts have mandatory accounts (managing settings, eventual billing). Optional post-session account creation for players who want history saved. Consent decoupled: session-level consent at join (lightweight, ToS plus AI processing notice) for anonymous players, full consent at account creation. Three tables: session_participants, accounts, consent_records. Architecture supports monster RPG persistent player identity without rebuild.

Decision: Service architecture: library plus thin API monolith at MVP
Rationale: Engine is a standalone Python library. API server is a thin HTTP wrapper. Same single deployed process, but engine has zero dependency on web framework. Enforces platform-clean discipline at type system level rather than relying on developer discipline. Alternatives: pure monolith (HTTP context leaks into engine), microservices (overkill for solo founder MVP).

Decision: Content events: structured semantic schema with presentation hints
Rationale: Each content event includes: event_type, actor_id, target, payload, presentation_hints (emotion, urgency, voice style, animation hint, lighting hint), target_audience, target_surface. Semantic events not pixel commands. Engine-friendly for downstream SDK consumption: TypeScript web SDK uses subset (text rendering); future Unity SDK uses full schema mapping presentation_hints to Animator parameters, AudioMixer settings, lighting cues. Same payload, different consumers. Engine never knows about display surfaces.

Decision: Knowledge graph data architecture: state tables plus append-only event log plus in-memory cache plus embedding-ready schema
Rationale: Pure SQL tables for current state. Append-only event log captures all changes (telemetry, replay, audit, recovery for free). In-memory cache during active sessions for sub-millisecond reads. Optional VECTOR(1536) columns on facts and events for future semantic features without migration. pgvector extension installed but not actively used at MVP. Apache AGE rejected at MVP: graph requirements do not justify complexity. Schema designed to add AGE later if monster RPG inference needs it.

Decision: Simulation harness: full harness for MVP
Rationale: AI-driven synthetic players (Llama 3 on Groq, near-free), seeded deterministic runs, scenario scripting for edge cases, batch run statistics, replay against changed engine versions. 1-2 weeks build. Force multiplier for solo founder iteration: synthetic sessions in minutes vs real playtests in hours. Without it, part-time build pace cannot ship Nightcap. Catches obvious failures but does not predict whether real groups enjoy a session. Real playtests still happen.

Decision: Compliance posture: GDPR-ready architecture at MVP, formal certifications deferred to enterprise ask
Rationale: GDPR is law in EU regardless of certification: any EU user playing Nightcap triggers compliance. Architecture choices that make GDPR possible are essentially free in chosen stack: encryption at rest (Cloud SQL default), encryption in transit (HTTPS), data minimization (discipline), right to deletion (foreign keys), consent tracking (consent_records table). Formal SOC 2 / ISO 27001 deferred until first enterprise customer specifically asks. HIPAA never (PRD declines clinical healthcare).

Decision: Model routing: LiteLLM SDK in-process at MVP
Rationale: LiteLLM is the dominant Python provider abstraction (40k stars, 140+ providers, Y Combinator backed, MIT licensed). SDK mode means no separate service to operate. Provider-agnostic routing through abstraction layer means switching Anthropic to Groq to anything else is a routing-table change. Migration to LiteLLM Proxy Server in H2 if external developers join. Custom abstraction rejected: distraction from core platform work.

Decision: Real-time event delivery: SSE plus POST with explicit transport adapter pattern
Rationale: Engine emits events to internal event bus (asyncio channels). Transport adapters subscribe and push to clients. MVP implements SSE adapter for Nightcap server push and POST endpoint for player input. WebSocket adapter added later when Couch online or Monster online require it. Adapter pattern means engine never changes when transports are added.

Decision: Stack positioning: Arcwright is narrative runtime middleware (Layer 2 in game dev stack)
Rationale: Arcwright sits between game engines (Unity, Unreal, Godot, web) and AI providers (Anthropic, OpenAI, Groq). Engine handles rendering, input, audio. Arcwright handles arc orchestration, characters, knowledge state, content events. AI providers handle generation. Engine-agnostic and provider-agnostic. Closest analogue: http://Charisma.ai (more controllable, character-level vs session-level). Differentiated from: Convai (3D-native lock-in), Inworld (pivoted), Latitude/Voyage (consumer not platform), Ink/Articy (authored not generative).

Decision: Visual scripting committed for H2/H3, schema designed for visual-builder-readiness now
Rationale: Visual scripting (Blueprints model) is a Tier 1 differentiator for designer/storyteller adoption. Authoring at MVP is YAML/TOML files plus Python extension hooks. Schema must be deterministic and graph-shaped (no imperative steps embedded; imperative logic goes in registered Python extensions with explicit interfaces). Visual builder UI is H2 or H3 priority work, built on top of the same schema. Schema is source of truth, visual editor is one possible UI.

Decision: Data architecture extends to locations, objects, decisions tables
Rationale: Story is more than dialogue. World state, object interaction, branch points are first-class. Schema additions: locations (id, identity, state, contained_object_ids), objects (id, identity, state, location_id, holder_character_id), decisions (id, context, options_offered, option_chosen, decided_by). Event types extended: object.created, object.moved, object.held, location.changed, environment.changed, decision.offered, decision.made. Nightcap MVP uses subset; monster RPG and couch co-op use all of it. Additive schema, no migration needed when more is used.

Decision: Cloud provider: GCP
Rationale: Best balance of dev velocity (Cloud Run is simplest serverless container platform), cost profile, and scale headroom for solo founder MVP. Enterprise credibility sufficient for H2 team-building deals. AWS rejected: more friction, GameLift Streams not relevant. Cloudflare Workers rejected: constrains language to TS/JS, time limits problematic for AI calls.

Decision: Six behavior commitments for AI characters (stochastic generation, initiative, NPC-NPC, goal pursuit, per-character profile, arc-level variability)
Rationale: (1) Behavior is generated not scripted (non-zero temperature, character voice). (2) Initiative scheduler: AI characters act unprompted based on goals and state. (3) NPC-to-NPC interactions are first-class. (4) Goal pursuit drives behavior, not just colors it. (5) Per-character behavior_profile JSONB column captures initiative threshold, goal pursuit weight, interaction style, variability. (6) Arc-level variability through non-deterministic decision points. These prevent over-predictability and support 'you had to be there' north star.

Decision: Arc execution engine: event-driven coordinator with Python asyncio plus python-statemachine v3, graph traversal
Rationale: Engine is event-driven coordinator: in-process pub/sub via asyncio, components subscribe and emit. python-statemachine v3.0 (Feb 2026) handles beat progression as graph traversal (branching, convergence, multiple endings, loops). Best fit for concurrent concerns (beat, pacing, initiative, character behavior, safety). Lowest engine overhead for player-perceived latency. Temporal rejected: heavyweight workflow engine designed for long-running cross-service durability, overkill for solo founder MVP with hour-long sessions.

Decision: Languages: Python for engine and API, TypeScript for web SDK and dashboard frontend
Rationale: Python: dominant ecosystem for AI orchestration (LiteLLM, Anthropic SDK, FastAPI, Pydantic). TypeScript: required for browser SDK and dashboard. Future engine-specific SDKs target their engine languages (C# for Unity, C++ for Unreal) in H2.

Decision: Primary database: Cloud SQL PostgreSQL
Rationale: In-region with Cloud Run (no cross-cloud latency). Real PostgreSQL with full extension support (pgvector, AGE if needed). $300 GCP free credits cover ~20 months of minimum config. Neon initially recommended but rejected: AWS-only as of May 2026, cross-cloud latency penalty 30-40ms per query. Migration to Neon possible if/when GCP support ships.

Decision: Build sequence philosophy: hybrid (build for Nightcap, design platform-clean from day one)
Rationale: Build only what Nightcap needs at MVP, but design every interface as platform-clean from line one. Avoids over-engineering a full platform pre-proof while preventing the 'extract platform later' anti-pattern. Forces discipline on layer separation. Alternatives considered: platform-first (too speculative for solo founder), Nightcap-only with later extraction (creates technical debt that becomes rewrites).

Decision: Authentication: Firebase Auth (dashboard) plus API keys (developers) plus short-lived JWTs (game clients)
Rationale: Three auth surfaces, three patterns. Firebase Auth handles dashboard human logins (GCP-native, free tier 50K MAU, includes password reset and MFA). API keys for developer backend code calling our API (industry standard). Short-lived JWTs for game clients authenticating to a specific session (issued at session start, scoped, expirable). Saves weeks of building auth flows from scratch.

Decision: Testing: focused unit tests on knowledge graph, arc engine, safety, model routing
Rationale: Solo founder plus Claude Code primary developer plus MVP scope means full TDD too slow and manual-only too risky. Critical correctness paths get unit tests; everything else manual at MVP. Integration tests added in H2 when external developers join. Specifically: knowledge graph correctness, arc state transitions, safety enforcement, model routing fallback get unit tests.

Decision: Arc structure: graph with branching, convergence, multiple endings, loops
Rationale: Arcs are not linear sequences. Beats are nodes; transitions are conditional edges. Graph supports: branching (multiple paths from a beat), convergence (multiple paths into a beat), loops (repeating beats like investigation rounds), multiple terminals (different endings), conditional transitions evaluated at runtime. World mutability supported through state JSONB on locations and objects. Arc mutation at runtime architecturally possible (data-driven YAML, not compiled), implementation deferred until use case requires it.

Decision: Content safety: three-layer at MVP, Layer 4 deferred with watchpoint
Rationale: Layer 1: deterministic hard stops (regex blocklists for absolute prohibited content). Layer 2: GPT-OSS-Safeguard 20B on Groq for pre-generation classification, with arc-defined policy (bring-your-own-policy). Layer 3: main LLM with policy in system prompt. Layer 4 (post-generation safety) deferred with watchpoint to add if real sessions surface output problems. Saves one safety call per turn at MVP. Murder mystery genre adjacent to dark content makes Layer 2 essential. GPT-OSS-Safeguard replaces deprecated Llama Guard 4 (Feb 2026 on Groq).

Decision: generation_logs full content population gated behind CONTENT_LOGGING_ENABLED feature flag (default false); non-PII fields populated at MVP
Rationale: Player-submitted content (names, character choices, dialogue) may appear in generation prompts. Storing prompt and output text before a GDPR consent architecture is in place creates compliance exposure. The table schema exists from day one with all columns including prompt_text TEXT NULL and output_text TEXT NULL. Non-PII fields (model, latency, tokens, cost, tension score) are populated at MVP. Full content population is deferred until GDPR consent architecture is in place and the flag is explicitly enabled.

Decision: Provenance chains adopted as first-class knowledge graph feature: every knowledge state record tracks why a character knows something, not just what they know
Rationale: Provenance chains (ordered list of character IDs from original source to current knower) enable contradiction detection, realistic 'I heard from someone that...' dialogue, deception modeling via confidence scores, and richer telemetry. Characters with short chains speak with certainty; characters at the end of long chains express appropriate uncertainty. Added to knowledge_states schema as provenance_chain JSONB array with confidence float. No schema migration required to use; it is present from day one.

Decision: Dramatic tension score adopted as unified first-class session metric driving pacing, misdirection, and model tier selection simultaneously
Rationale: A single composite float (0.0-1.0) computed from time pressure, player action rate, correct suspicion confidence, and clue coverage eliminates three separate threshold systems and creates a cleaner architecture. When tension peaks, the routing layer automatically upgrades character dialogue to premium tier without any explicit call-site code. Weights are arc-configurable in pacing_config, enabling iteration without code changes.

Decision: python-statemachine v3.0 StateChart confirmed as arc execution library; StateChart base class (not StateMachine) is correct
Rationale: Verified May 11 2026 that v3.0 supports all required arc graph patterns: parallel regions (State.Parallel), compound states (State.Compound), history states, delayed events, conditional transitions via cond=, and invoked sub-machines. The StateChart base class is the SCXML-compliant class introduced in v3.0; StateMachine is the legacy v2.x-compatible class and must not be used. NightcapArcChart implements investigation beat as State.Parallel so private clue distribution and group interrogation run simultaneously.

Decision: Nightcap character mode: generated. Characters are generated per session from typed character slots, not from authored fixed definitions. Character identities, names, and personalities are generated at session start, calibrated to the specific player group.
Rationale: Every session should feel like a genuinely different story (Miracle Workers / Glass Onion model). Fixed authored characters produce a recognizable cast that repeats, reducing replayability. The arc schema supports authored, generated, and hybrid modes at the platform level; Nightcap uses generated.

Decision: Nightcap setting constraint: social gathering. The physical construct of every Nightcap session is some kind of social gathering. Time period, tone, and specific setting vary within this constraint.
Rationale: Players should not need heavy imaginative lifting to inhabit the world. Social gathering is something all players have real-world experience with. Time period and accent can vary freely. Exotic settings with no player reference point break immersion rather than building it.

Decision: Nightcap aesthetic mode: generative. The visual and tonal aesthetic of each session is generated fresh. The platform supports fixed, palette, and generative aesthetic modes; Nightcap uses generative.
Rationale: Generative aesthetics contribute to session unrepeatability. Future arcs can use any mode.

Decision: Nightcap play mode at MVP: imposter. One player is assigned the killer role at session start and plays to avoid detection. All other players are investigators. The killer receives a fundamentally different private phone experience from all other players.
Rationale: Imposter mode creates the strongest social dynamics for a group party game: paranoia, misdirection, alliance-forming, and accusation. The killer's private experience maps directly to the personalization engine's strength. Replay value is high because the killer role is randomly assigned each session. Detective race mode deferred to v1.1.

Decision: Nightcap arc structure: Dan Harmon Story Circle. Arc structure is a configurable platform-level parameter; Nightcap uses Dan Harmon.
Rationale: The Story Circle maps cleanly onto a murder mystery: normal world, disruption (murder), investigation, revelation, return transformed. Man in a Hole works for individual player emotional arcs within a session and can coexist at that level.

Decision: Nightcap narrator: host persona on shared display. Voiced character on the TV. Aesthetic-linked persona, omniscient, player-addressable. Not a murder suspect. Behavior triggers: beat transitions, clue releases, tension threshold, player inaction. Mostly absent from phones.
Rationale: The narrator is the architect of the evening: the host who built the stage and knows everything. The TV is the social anchor all players share when dispersed on phones. Persona adapts to aesthetic (dry Victorian butler vs theatrical Italian host) contributing to unrepeatability. Not a competing detective but an all-knowing master of ceremonies.

Decision: Story Circle is a functional platform-native 8-beat template (not decorative metadata)
Rationale: arc_structure field value changed from dan_harmon to story_circle. Story Circle is now a first-class platform object that scaffolds 8 pre-populated beat containers with story_circle_step, structural_function, and suggested dramatic_purpose. arc_structure is a functional engine input used for generation context, pacing calibration, and structural intent. Authors using Story Circle get pre-scaffolded beats; custom arc authors define their own. Resolved the open architecture question from Chat 7 pre-work.

Decision: Killer active capabilities are story-prompted optional actions, not tracked resource pools
Rationale: No ability_charge concept. The engine identifies when a story moment makes an action narratively appropriate and presents it as an optional prompt on the killer device. Killer accepts or declines. Story continues either way. The killer's experience is being a character in a story, not managing a hand of cards. Ability_pool concept in arc definition now means the set of action types available in this session, not a resource counter.

Decision: Session duration is emergent (30-75 min target range). No separate speed round mode.
Rationale: Speed round is a fast group plus responsive pacing engine, not a designed configuration. Duration range replaces any session_mode parameter. The pacing engine manages within the 30-75 minute range based on player behavior. Both ends of the range are valid session outcomes.

Decision: Nightcap uses 8 execution beats in 1:1 Story Circle mapping: The Arrival, The Body, The Opening Move, The Dig, The Thread, The Reckoning, The Close, The Truth
Rationale: 8 beats gives the engine direct knowledge of the current Story Circle phase at all times, enabling structural intent injection into generation, tone-aware pacing, and ensemble coordination. Named beats are Nightcap-canonical labels, separate from the Story Circle step names. BeatDefinition schema expanded with story_circle_step, structural_function, dramatic_purpose, emotional_target, information_goal, tension_target, character_emphasis.

Decision: aesthetic_mode replaced by aesthetic_config with per-element generation strategies. tone_config added to arc definition.
Rationale: Aesthetic has four categories: host-selectable (era + occasion, with Pick for Me option), pre-produced per theme (backgrounds, music, animations, UI), generated at session start (character portraits), and generative runtime (all text/narrative). Pre-produced is the v1 implementation; runtime generation is a planned A/B experiment. tone_config has brand_envelope (outer bounds per dimension), scenario_defaults (dial positions), and voice_directive (free text). Nightcap brand envelope: irreverence 0.5-1.0, suspense 0.4-0.9, dark comedy 0.4-0.85, wit density 0.6-1.0, chaos tolerance 0.3-0.8.

Decision: Fully generative story content is a Nightcap-specific design decision, not a platform requirement
Rationale: The Arcwright platform supports authored, generative, and hybrid content strategies per arc. Nightcap chose fully generative story elements (characters, murder, clues, dialogue). Other arcs may have fixed authored characters, authored story content with generative edges, or any other combination. This distinction must be explicit in all platform documentation to avoid miscommunicating platform capabilities as Nightcap design choices.

Decision: Victim is a dedicated 7th structural position. Killer designates victim at revelation. Eligibility governed by player count (threshold: 4+ human players for player-eligible).
Rationale: Victim slot is separate from the 6 player role types. In standard player counts (under threshold), victim is NPC. With 4+ human players, a human can be designated. The killer selects from available characters at the moment of revelation. Victim player receives their own private revelation with role assignment from the victim role pool: Witness, Specter, Informant, or Conspirator (conditional). All victim roles keep the player engaged through Beat 8. All learn killer identity at Beat 8 with everyone else.

Decision: Balance principle is an engine-enforced constraint: clue chain always sufficient for full investigator engagement, interference throttled against balance state
Rationale: The session must remain genuinely winnable for both sides at all times. Genuine clue chain has enough redundancy that no single interference event makes the case unsolvable. Killer interference opportunities are throttled against the session balance state. False clues are always falsifiable given the complete genuine evidence set. This principle governs clue distribution, puzzle gating, and killer action opportunity surfacing.

Decision: Killer revelation is mid-session, variable between beats 2-4. Killer assignment and killer revelation are two distinct events.
Rationale: Killer does not know they are the killer at session start. Assignment fires internally at any point from session start to just before revelation. Engine observes player behavior passively and can be accelerated by specific interaction triggers. Revelation fires privately on killer device at assigned beat (range 2-4). This produces authentic early-game investigative behavior and a strategic pivot moment when revelation lands. Differentiates Nightcap from all major imposter-format games.

Decision: Murder timing is variable across beats 1-3. Mini-games active in beats 1-3 feed killer assignment logic.
Rationale: Murder can occur in any of the first three beats, not at a fixed narrative point. This mimics real social dynamics and creates meaningfully different session experiences. Mini-games (Jackbox-style social games) are framed as part of the gathering and run in beats 1-3. Their behavioral outputs are named inputs to the killer assignment logic. Players have no visibility into this function.

Decision: D-014 Signature emotional outcomes ranked: narrative pull (primary), world surprise that lets player feel done (secondary), creature uniqueness (tertiary).
Rationale: Creature is texture; story is draw.

Decision: D-024 No fixed limit on bonded creatures over a save. Bond depth varies (deep lifelong plus lighter chapter-of-life).
Rationale: Genre breadth and design depth coexist.

Decision: D-021 Standard difficulty scaling, not multi-axis subsystem.
Rationale: Use platform's existing capability per PRD §5; don't reinvent.

Decision: D-025 Active party size: 3-4 creatures maximum.
Rationale: Each creature gets real screen time; distinguishes from Pokemon's 6.

Decision: D-030 Monster RPG player-facing term is 'chapter,' 'session' is platform/technical terminology.
Rationale: Player-facing language matches life-and-story framing.

Decision: D-003 Monster RPG world runs in story-time, not real-time or paused.
Rationale: Removes absence pressure for working adults; aligns with life-and-fate framing.

Decision: D-005 Witness Bond as foundational creature relationship model.
Rationale: Most platform-leveraged of four candidate models; best fit for 'uniquely mine' signature outcome.

Decision: D-016 Player age default: young adult or undefined, no fixed number, no visible aging markers.
Rationale: Player imagination fills in; supports apprenticeship framing.

Decision: D-020 Levels and XP retained for creature progression as a layer separate from Witness Bond.
Rationale: Familiar player feedback supported by SDT research; Witness Bond carries identity, levels carry capability.

Decision: D-008 Creature autonomy enforced. Bonding consensual; departure allowed; refusal real.
Rationale: Brand position; engine enforces.

Decision: D-004 Career path system: mentor-gated discovery, reversible, see-all-and-commit narrative pressure.
Rationale: Core Monster RPG differentiator; mirrors real-life career discovery patterns.

Decision: D-018 No gossip propagation between NPCs as a platform mechanism. World consequences flow through procedural fate.
Rationale: Procedural fate generator already handles 'world responds at scale.'

Decision: D-011 Adaptive emergent session closure with narrative_momentum_score pacing model.
Rationale: Best fit for life-and-fate framing; design philosophy matters more than complexity cost.

Decision: D-019 Creature-as-social-radar dropped as separate mechanism. Witness Bond consequence preserved.
Rationale: Avoid duplicate mechanisms; preserve natural Witness Bond behavior.

Decision: D-028 Procedural responsive fate generator at full MVP scope, not deferred to Phase 2.
Rationale: PRD §5 commitment deserves full version; secondary signature outcome depends on it.

Decision: D-022 Thread genealogy. Career arcs can spawn child career arcs via branching beats.
Rationale: Serves career path differentiator; produces narrative coherence over a long save.

Decision: D-010 Player cannot perform cruelty toward creatures or NPCs as a gameplay mechanic.
Rationale: Brand position; engine enforces.

Decision: D-029 Monster RPG Story Bible structured as 12 chapters.
Rationale: Identity, life-and-fate, world, characters, creatures, careers, narrative, pacing, narrator, rules, content territory, example.

Decision: D-001 Monster RPG single-player at MVP. Multiplayer deferred to a later phase.
Rationale: Reduces MVP scope; matches founder intent for the core experience.

Decision: D-026 Home base as a player-established location type. Bonded creatures live somewhere comfortable.
Rationale: Avoids storage/object framing; respects creature autonomy.

Decision: D-023 NPC memory: narrative-meaningful events only, not every micro-interaction.
Rationale: Bounded knowledge graph load; PRD §5 cross-session memory commitment preserved at meaningful depth.

Decision: D-012 No omniscient narrator in Monster RPG. Dialogue plus character POV moments. NarratorConfig: none.
Rationale: Player-respecting; matches literary-fiction approach.

Decision: D-015 Found family default. Player character starts without family; family of choice develops through play.
Rationale: Emotional and narrative richness; universally resonant pattern.

Decision: D-027 Travel rest space (design-original, not pokeball-shaped). Creatures use by choice.
Rationale: Practical mechanic respecting creature autonomy and IP boundaries.

Decision: D-006 Pact terms simplified to 'current wanting' state, not renegotiable contract object.
Rationale: Same behavioral effect at lower schema complexity.

Decision: D-002 Couch co-op is a separate (third) game candidate, Hazelight-influenced.
Rationale: Distinct design and technical needs from Monster RPG; sequencing serially is optimal.

Decision: D-013 Creatures and NPCs age over story-time; player character does not age.
Rationale: Preserves open-ended campaign; world mortality carries fate's emotional weight.

Decision: D-007 Inheritance hooks dropped as a separate creature mechanism. Prior creature history is authored backstory only.
Rationale: Did not earn its complexity. Regular Witness Bond produces relationship depth.

Decision: D-017 No reputation as a separate concept. Per-NPC opinions only. World-level consequences via procedural responsive fate.
Rationale: Network of NPC opinions IS reputation; separate aggregate is unnecessary.

Decision: D-009 Non-lethal competition. No creature death from battles.
Rationale: Brand position; engine enforces; differentiates from Pokemon's implicit ambiguity.

Decision: D-035 Visual Storyworld staged roadmap
Rationale: Visual creator tooling, internally referred to as Visual Storyworld, is pulled forward from H3 to a five-phase staged roadmap. Phase 1 (inspection surfaces: live knowledge graph visualization, read-only arc structure view, live event stream, character state inspection) ships with platform MVP in H1. Phase 2 (structured editing of existing primitives: arc composition, character authoring, content event templates) ships during H2 prep. Phase 3 (live preview and runtime debugging) ships with H2 launch. Phase 4 (full visual flow editor, Blueprints-shape) ships H2 mature to H3 early. Phase 5 (asset libraries, marketplace if pull exists, AI-assisted authoring) ships H3 plus. Positioning is narrative middleware with extraordinary creator tooling, not no-code platform. Phase ordering may be adjusted based on customer learning and bandwidth signals.

Decision: D-032 Brand approach is soft bifurcation
Rationale: One legal entity. Two product brands inside it. Arcwright (or Arcwright Engine, final platform brand name deferred to dedicated naming chat) is the platform-facing identity for developers and enterprise. Arcwright Studios is the consumer-facing publishing label for games. Platform-facing web properties, documentation, enterprise contracts, and developer materials lead with the platform brand. Studio brand appears on game splash screens, App Store and Steam pages, consumer site, and press for game releases. Two trademark filings inside one corporate entity. Decision absorbs the audit's brand-neutrality concern while preserving operational simplicity for a solo founder.

Decision: D-033 D-022 revised under soft bifurcation
Rationale: D-022 (company name is Arcwright Studios) is revised: parent entity name is Arcwright (or whatever legal name is chosen with counsel) and Arcwright Studios is the publishing sub-brand within that entity. Specific legal-name decision deferred to legal-formation work.

Decision: D-031 H2 external beta gate updated to require Nightcap profitability
Rationale: H2 external developer beta is contingent on Nightcap profitability signal in addition to the existing five external developers building non-Nightcap experiences gate. Specific profitability threshold (revenue level, unit economics, recurring revenue) is deferred until Nightcap pricing model is set. This is a hybrid posture absorbing one of the closer-to-Lighthouse audit guardrails without abandoning the Pixar to Valve to Stripe progression. Supersedes prior H2 gate language in PRD Section 9.

Decision: D-034 Wedge is capability-shaped, not segment-shaped
Rationale: The Arcwright wedge is a capability, not a customer demographic. The wedge capability is cross-session narrative state management with knowledge graph as the headline primitive. Customer segments self-select by need rather than being recruited by demographic. THE tool for ambitious narrative games is the 5 to 10 year destination, not the H2 target. Implications: documentation, API design, marketing positioning, and partnership conversations lead with cross-session state and knowledge graph as the value proposition. Characters, arcs, pacing, fate, and content rails are supporting cast in how the platform talks about itself.

Decision: D-038 Schema naming principle (standing rule)
Rationale: Schema names describe structure. Game-specific semantics go in configuration. All current and future schema renames must satisfy this principle. The schema is the platform's external surface to developers; clean schemas are load-bearing for extreme ease of use.

Decision: D-040 Build path reaffirmed and clarified
Rationale: D-059 (build for Nightcap, design platform-clean) stands. Platform-clean means clean internal abstractions and game-agnostic schemas, not external API exposure during H1. External developer exposure is gated by closer-to-Lighthouse plus the updated H2 profitability gate per D-031. The audit's generalized API engine concern is addressed by external-exposure timing, not by abandoning internal architectural discipline. Refactoring debt from a monolith-first approach would fall hardest when Monster RPG ships in H2, which is unacceptable for a solo founder.

Decision: D-036 TypeScript clarification (reaffirmation and extension of D-057)
Rationale: TypeScript is correct for the web SDK and dashboard frontend. Engine SDKs (Unity in C#, Unreal in C++ with Blueprints integration, Godot in GDScript or C#, native mobile in Swift and Kotlin if pursued) require their native languages and are separate engineering efforts wrapping the same REST API. Backend Python remains correct for the AI-heavy engine. The phrase 'the SDK' is plural; each engine SDK is its own real piece of work.

Decision: D-037 Business model architecture (four tiers, narrative-action credits)
Rationale: Four pricing tiers: Free Developer, Indie, Studio, and Enterprise. Action credits are abstracted as narrative actions rather than raw tokens or API calls. Per-project pricing is default at Studio tier with per-seat available. Revenue share remains a configurable enterprise option, not the default model. Specific numeric prices deferred until Nightcap pricing is set. Extends and completes D-034.

Decision: D-039 Schema renames pass 1 (seven renames)
Rationale: Seven renames confirmed: bonded_creatures to bonded_entities; home_base_location to player_anchor_location; career_path to player_role_arc; active_party to current_companion_entities; agency_vs_fate event tag to event_authorship with initial enum {player, world}; witnessing_creature_ids to witness_entity_ids; pact_term to current_intent, with historical wants stored separately in intent_history. Renames propagate through API surface, TypeScript SDK type definitions, dashboard UI labels, documentation, example code, Monster RPG Bible, and Nightcap Bible.

Decision: D-042 Witness Bond scope at MVP
Rationale: Witness-event tagging in the knowledge graph ships at MVP because it is core to the capability-wedge and Nightcap benefits from it directly. Multi-dimensional bond_state schema is defined from day one. MVP uses a single-dimensional bond depth scalar. Full multi-dimensional implementation ships at Monster RPG H2.

Decision: D-041 MVP scope pattern (schema-clean, implementation-staged)
Rationale: Schema design is platform-clean from day one and includes all fields needed for full functionality. Implementation is staged. Nightcap MVP gets minimum-viable implementations of advanced features. Monster RPG H2 fills in sophisticated implementations. Schema does not change between MVP and H2. This synthesizes platform-clean and closer-to-Lighthouse: schema-clean preserves platform discipline, implementation-narrow respects solo-founder bandwidth.

Decision: D-045 narrative_momentum_score scope at MVP
Rationale: The narrative_momentum_score function exists in the API at MVP with simplified implementation: event-count plus arc-position awareness, suggesting pause after N events or predefined arc breakpoints. Sophisticated multi-signal scoring with cross-session pacing memory and closing beat detection ships at Monster RPG H2.

Decision: D-043 Procedural fate generator scope at MVP
Rationale: Fate event schema and template engine ship at MVP. Implementation is condition-driven templates only. Full procedural multi-signal generator ships at Monster RPG H2 when long-form RPG signals are exercised.

Decision: D-044 Thread genealogy scope at MVP
Rationale: The parent_thread_id field exists on the thread schema from day one, nullable, defaulting to null. Genealogy traversal logic using parent_thread_id for narrative coherence over long saves ships at Monster RPG H2. Nightcap is single-session and does not exercise thread ancestry.

Decision: D-047 DRAFT Enterprise wedge hypothesis (working hypothesis, not committed)
Rationale: Per user signal that enterprise is too early to discuss, this is retained as a draft for revisit, not a hard commitment. Working hypothesis: Arcwright's enterprise wedge is a white-label-first, direct-secondary corporate team-building offering, sold primarily through mid-tier events vendors and secondarily to HR or People Ops decision-makers at companies of 200 or more employees. Testable within 12 months of Nightcap consumer launch by recruiting at least two events vendors as white-label partners, running at least three multi-session corporate engagements, and achieving either repeat booking rate above 50 percent or aggregate first-year revenue above $50K from these partnerships. Falsification conditions include buyer preference for fresh content over continuity, no vendor willingness after five qualified conversations, or cost-per-session economics failing to match category alternatives.

Decision: D-046 Enterprise product structure (portfolio of arcs on same platform)
Rationale: Arcwright's enterprise team-building offering is a portfolio of arc templates running on the same Arcwright platform that powers Nightcap consumer. Arcs for enterprise are purpose-built (leadership development, team formation, onboarding narratives) or adapted from Nightcap. The platform is the same; the products are distinct. Resolves the open question about enterprise as distinct arc type vs same product with different rails.

Decision: D-048 M0 Wizard-of-Oz validation gate overridden
Rationale: PRD v1.3 Section 9 defined a manual Wizard-of-Oz facilitation phase (minimum 10 sessions, personalization perception signal required in 2+) as a required gate before significant production engineering. This gate is explicitly overridden. The founder's build sequence is: Engine, Platform, Game, live test with real users, iterate. No game exists yet and no one has played a session. Validation of the personalization thesis will happen through real-user qualifying sessions at M6, not a pre-engineering simulation phase. The personalization thesis is treated as a working hypothesis to be confirmed or disproved by the real product in real users' hands. This is consistent with the PRD's explicit founder-override provision. Risk accepted: production engineering begins before the personalization layer has been validated with real users. If M6 live testing reveals the personalization thesis does not hold, more engineering will need to be revisited than if a WoZ phase had surfaced this earlier. Founder accepts this tradeoff explicitly.
Tags: M0, validation, wizard-of-oz, MVP, override, personalization

Decision: D-049 GitHub Projects and Issues is the primary project tracker for the Arcwright build
Rationale: Code, AI agents (Claude Code, Codex, Copilot), and task tracking live in one place. Agents can read issues, execute against them, open PRs, and close issues via commit message references natively. No integration bridge is required between code and tracker. GitHub sub-issues and milestones support the roadmap's milestone, epic, and task hierarchy. Zero admin overhead for a solo founder. Linear and Jira were considered and rejected: Linear adds context-switching cost without proportionate benefit at this team size; Jira is designed for multi-team process governance that does not yet exist. Revisit trigger: first non-technical co-founder or PM hire who needs richer reporting or lives in a different tool.

Decision: D-050 Nightcap game UI will be built on an external platform connecting to Arcwright via API
Rationale: The Nightcap experience layer (host interface, player interface) will be delivered via a third-party platform that connects to Arcwright through its API, rather than Arcwright building those surfaces directly. This is consistent with the PRD surface-agnosticism principle: the engine emits content events tagged with context; the experience layer renders them. The specific external platform is not yet named. Roadmap implication: M4 task decomposition is deferred until the external platform is selected because that choice drives the required SDK or integration work. M1 through M3 are unaffected because they are backend and API only. Revisit when M3 nears its exit gate.

Decision: D-051 Nightcap Continuity committed to v1.1
Rationale: Nightcap Continuity, defined as cross-session group memory plus a post-session recap artifact, is committed to v1.1 rather than v1. v1 remains focused on proving the complete single-session party game. v1.1 becomes the first fast-follow that demonstrates the D-034 wedge: cross-session narrative state management with the knowledge graph as the headline primitive. The v1.1 scope includes recap generation after The Truth, optional group memory reuse at future session creation, host controls for reuse/exclusion/deletion, and privacy-aware retention. Continuity must personalize future generated content without allowing AI to manage canonical session state or bypass deterministic arc execution. Durable approval evidence also lives in ADR docs/decisions/0006-nightcap-continuity-v11.md.
Tags: Nightcap, continuity, v1.1, D-034, ADR-0006, knowledge-graph, recap

Decision: D-052 Nightcap v1 player floor is four human players
Rationale: Nightcap v1 requires four human players and scales up with additional human players. Interrogatable AI participants that fill empty player slots, hold private knowledge state, and can be the killer are deferred to v1.1. The narrator, NPC victim role, and non-participating background narration remain in v1. This resolves the prior PRD 4-10 versus Story Bible 2-8 player-count conflict in favor of a four-player v1 floor. Rationale: interrogatable AI participants are an unscoped engine feature with hardest-session-level complexity; the four-human floor preserves the full four-slot core experience without that scope.

Decision: D-053 Nightcap arc is canonically eight Story Circle beats
Rationale: Nightcap uses eight Story Circle beats. Beat count is an arc-level property, not a platform or engine property. Monster RPG uses emergent arc_structure with no fixed beat count, and future games choose their own structure. No documentation may attribute a fixed beat count to the platform or engine. The prior three-beat shorthand in the PRD and roadmap is stale and must be corrected. Rationale: the eight-beat arc is the detailed Story Bible design; three-beat shorthand risks being wrongly implemented as a three-state machine.

Decision: D-054 Personalization intake added to Nightcap v1 scope
Rationale: Nightcap v1 includes three host seed questions at session creation plus one to two player prompts at join. The engine uses these inputs to calibrate character tensions, generated character fit, and narrator callbacks. Rationale: the personalization perception gate cannot fire without group-specific inputs, and anonymous sub-30-second join supplies none.

Decision: D-055 Nightcap Continuity remains v1.1 and learns from past sessions
Rationale: Nightcap Continuity is committed to v1.1, not v1, to avoid disrupting in-flight work. Continuity includes cross-session group memory, narrator callbacks to prior sessions, and shareable post-session recap. v1.1 continuity should also learn from a group's past sessions to deepen intake personalization over time. Rationale: this demonstrates the D-034 cross-session-state wedge in H1 without disrupting current milestones.

Decision: D-056 M5 second arc upgraded to executable second arc after Nightcap M6 proof
Rationale: The M5 second arc schema deliverable is upgraded from designed-only to an executable second arc sequenced after Nightcap M6 proof. The written schema remains the design document, then becomes a minimal real product. Working concept: a solo daily single-suspect interrogation game where the suspect remembers prior days through the knowledge graph. Rationale: executable reuse proves the core platform claim more strongly than design assertion while protecting the Nightcap timeline by sequencing it after proof.

Decision: D-057 Nightcap pricing north star is host-pays-per-session
Rationale: Nightcap's temporary pricing north star is host-pays-per-session, anchored above the roughly $30 static murder-mystery-kit price point. Credit packs are a later retention or average-order optimization, pursued only if repeat purchase appears. The engine must support per-session charging and per-session cost tracking. The specific numeric launch price remains a launch-time decision informed by real per-session AI cost and early willingness-to-pay signal. This working north star may change as session uniqueness is proven and iterated. Rationale: per-session is the simplest willingness-to-pay test and matches the proof-signal framework; second purchase is the real signal.

Decision: D-058 Mini-games are a core Nightcap v1 interaction layer
Rationale: Mini-games are a core interaction layer in Nightcap v1, not an opening-only feature. v1 includes timed puzzle-gated mini-games across beats that gate clue access, drive competition, and create investigative leads, built on the existing puzzle-gated clue architecture from Story Bible Section 7. Behavioral-read output is captured in schema but not wired into killer assignment in v1; v1 killer assignment remains constrained-random behind the existing assignment interface. v1.1 wires mini-game behavioral signals into killer assignment, expands the mini-game suite, and adds cross-campaign behavioral reads. Rationale: timed competitive clue-cracking throughout is the protected differentiator; invisible behavioral-to-assignment plumbing and suite expansion are the deferrable cost.
Tags: Nightcap, mini-games, clues, killer-assignment, v1, v1.1

Decision: D-059 Mini-game runtime and authoring boundary
Rationale: Mini-game packages use stable per-game paths, semantic versions, and draft, playtest, active, or retired lifecycle metadata. Python owns authoritative timing, submissions, scoring, outcomes, clue unlocking, and future persistence. Web clients render authorized state and submit actions only. v1 captures neutral metrics and deterministic game-scoped observations without using them for killer assignment or cross-session behavior. Every game defines a delayed clue fallback so failure cannot make the case unsolvable. Durable architecture evidence lives in ADR docs/decisions/0009-mini-game-runtime-boundary.md.
Tags: Nightcap, mini-games, architecture, lifecycle, behavioral-reads, clue-fallback, ADR-0009

Decision: D-060 Mini-game arc position constraint removed. Mini-games are no longer restricted to Beats 1 through 3. They may fire at any beat
Rationale: or serve as the primary experience structure.
Status: Opening the design surface to allow mini-games to operate throughout the arc, including as session-anchoring experiences rather than pre-investigation warmups. Behavioral signal function is preserved for pre-assignment games. Post-assignment games serve defined session functions instead.

Decision: D-061 First production Nightcap mini-game authored as an explicit precursor to AW-254
Rationale: AW-254 rehearsal requires a founder-selected production game ID, but no production mini-game package exists: the repository holds only non-shipping _fixtures (lifecycle playtest) and _template (lifecycle draft), and no decision record names a production game. Promoting a fixture is forbidden (do not activate non-shipping fixtures) and inventing content inside the rehearsal task is forbidden (no production content without founder approval). Founder direction is to author the first production game as an explicit precursor task (AW-257) using the AW-249 authoring foundation and AW-250 content and safety contracts, including an authored delayed clue fallback, before AW-254 promotes and rehearses it. AW-254 gains a dependency on AW-257 and stays blocked until the package is authored, reviewed, and founder-approved. Behavioral output stays schema-captured and unused for killer assignment in v1.
Tags: Nightcap, mini-games, production-game, AW-254, AW-257, precursor, clue-fallback

Decision: D-062 Crime Scene Smash and Evidence Locker approved as the first production Nightcap mini-game packages
Rationale: Crime Scene Smash is approved as the first production Nightcap mini-game package to be authored under AW-257. The package is a Nightcap-tailored match-3 clue gate with per-player boards, a shared leaderboard, and a reduced-clue fallback so the mystery cannot dead-end. The engine-supported participation mode remains individual, with table competition expressed through leaderboard and score rules rather than a new schema enum. This approval provides durable product evidence for the package and keeps the implementation aligned with the current mini-game schema boundary. Evidence Locker #402 added to the approved production set on June 27, 2026 per D-064 two-rehearsal sequence so Rehearsal 1 exercises both a multi-player (Crime Scene Smash) and a solo (Evidence Locker) mechanic.
Tags: Nightcap, mini-games, production-game, AW-257, crime-scene-smash, evidence-locker, clue-fallback, participation-mode

Decision: D-063 Tell Me Something True approved as a Nightcap social opener mini-game design candidate
Rationale: Founder direction selects Tell Me Something True as an approved Nightcap social opener and future mini-game design candidate. The locked design lives in docs/specs/0061-aw-258-tell-me-something-true.md. This decision approves the game ID and design direction for AW-258, not final shipped package content and not a replacement for the first production package decision in D-062. Crime Scene Smash remains the first production Nightcap mini-game package under AW-257 and AW-254. Any future Tell Me Something True package must include an authored delayed clue fallback where the mini-game schema requires it, pass content, asset, safety, and schema review, and receive founder content sign-off before authoring is marked complete. Behavioral output stays schema-captured and unused for killer assignment or cross-session behavior in v1.
Tags: Nightcap, mini-games, social-opener, AW-258, tell-me-something-true, clue-fallback

Decision: Proposed gameplay pivots for post-playtest evaluation
Rationale: Formal documentation of two potential future gameplay models for Nightcap (Couch Game and Competitive Investigator) to ensure they are evaluated against playtest feedback.

Decision: D-064 Two-rehearsal sequence for Nightcap mini-games
Rationale: Crime Scene Smash and Evidence Locker promoted for Rehearsal 1 (M4 close). Tell Me Something True implemented and rehearsed separately as Rehearsal 2 in M5-F. Splits a multi-week effort into a 1-week M4 close plus a sequenced M5 epic so the founder can run a real-human session this week and feed its blockers into TMST work.

Decision: D-065 Local-tunnel deployment for Rehearsal 1
Rationale: Rehearsal 1 runs on local Docker (engine plus Postgres) plus cloudflared tunnel exposing the web app to player phones. Cloud deployment deferred until AW-261 records the ADR-0003 validation outcome and a follow-on M5 task provisions it. Avoids coupling first-real-human session to cloud-infrastructure setup risk.

Decision: D-066 Polish tier split for Rehearsal 1
Rationale: Tier 1 polish (engineering correctness, performance, M4 exit-gate criteria, basic UX states, accessibility) is the Rehearsal 1 bar. Tier 2 polish (visual identity, art, animation, sound, brand system) deferred to M5-G epic. Rationale: rehearsal exists to surface blockers; investing in Tier 2 before any human has played wastes design budget on the wrong moments.

Decision: D-067 ADR-0003 Cloudflare vs GCP validation outcome
Rationale: Stay with Cloudflare as the canonical Nightcap web-experience runtime. Cost, edge coordination via Workers plus hibernatable Durable Objects, and free-tier suitability for MVP volume outweigh cross-vendor auth bridging cost. Comparison recorded in docs/decisions/0003-nightcap-web-experience-runtime.md Validation Outcome section. Implementation lives in AW-269.

Decision: D-068 Dissertation-derived platform improvements adopted
Rationale: Founder adopted three improvements from comparing dissertation working draft v0.7 'Story Logic Before Story Language' against canonical docs: (1) optional authorial_intent block in ArcDefinition with theme, tone, and per-beat emotional targets, plus realized-versus-intended tension telemetry; (2) narrative obligations model with a generic all_mandatory_obligations_resolved session condition, closing the untracked generative-misdirection coherence gap; (3) continuity and coherence eval suite on the existing eval harness and headless runner. Authoring-time approval workflows, AI creative roles, and intent versioning explicitly not adopted per PRD non-goals. Post-generation continuity classifier and host-facing rollback logged as open questions only. Full record: docs/decisions/0012-authorial-intent-obligations-continuity-evals.md.

Decision: D-069 Narrative content pipeline scoped into M5 before Rehearsal 1
Rationale: Synthetic execution proof (docs/roadmap/operations/0068-content-pass-findings.md v1.1) showed the canonical session loop emits zero narrative text: 24 events all structural, content_text empty, harness forbids LLM calls and still completes. Founder approved scoping minimum viable narrative into M5 ahead of Rehearsal 1: AW-276 voice block injection (F1, Hard Rules prompt approval granted), AW-277 narrator beat-transition lines, AW-278 killer revelation private sequence, AW-279 character identity delivery, AW-280 clue release content. Acceptance: re-run of the event-dump verification shows narrative content_text at beat turns, revelation, identity delivery, and clue release.

Decision: D-070 Story presentation is animation plus audio plus text
Rationale: Founder directive: the Nightcap story experience is animation with audio, not text-only. Narrative moments are staged audiovisual sequences. Implementation contract: narrative content events must carry presentation hints (voice_hint, animation_hint, lighting_hint, pause_before_ms already on ContentEvent) from their first implementation so game layers can stage animation and audio; engine stays surface-agnostic per architecture principle 1. Web layer renders animation via spec 0069 seq-* sequences; audio staging (0068 s6 stinger set) is promoted from post-M6 candidate to directional scope, sequenced per D-066 polish tiers (Tier 2 execution remains M5-G/M6 window).

Decision: D-071 Nightcap Couch Race becomes the v1 launch target
Rationale: Founder direction accepts and executes the ADR-0010 pivots, combined, before Rehearsal 1: Nightcap v1 is a 2-8 player, 20-40 minute couch race on TV plus phones where all players are investigators racing to solve a murder committed by an AI suspect. Killer/victim/method/motive/clue web resolve deterministically at session start; AI composes suspect dialogue from resolved knowledge state. Interrogation (menu-driven question intents, knowledge-gated answers, claim ledger, deterministic contradiction detection) becomes shared platform capability with Daily Case. Competition structure is an arc-configurable dial; v1 ships the solo race. Killer-among-players is renamed the Imposter Variant and stays approved future scope. Rehearsal 1 retargets to a Couch Race thin slice. Full record: docs/decisions/0013-nightcap-couch-race-v1-launch-target.md; canonical bible: docs/story-bibles/nightcap-couch-race.md.
Tags: nightcap, gameplay, product, roadmap, adr:0013, couch-race

Decision: D-072 AI character dialogue joins the live session loop for Rehearsal 1
Rationale: Founder decision: the AI character dialogue stack (knowledge-constrained generation, initiative targeting, social pressure) is wired into the live REST session loop rather than remaining harness-only for Rehearsal 1. A player dialogue input may trigger at most one AI character response, generated through the safe generate() entry point with the arc's content rails and authorial intent, published on the session event bus. Hosts can seat AI-controlled characters into a session. Deterministic-first ordering preserved: arc state advances before any generation call. Spec: docs/specs/0071-live-loop-ai-character-dialogue.md. Note: this capability is a stated dependency of D-071 (Couch Race suspect-answer generation).

Decision: D-073 Nightcap art direction brief proposed pending founder discovery
Rationale: PR #243 contains candidate research for AW-267, not approved by the founder. Founder discovery, comparative direction options, artifact review, revision, and explicit final sign-off remain pending. The candidate material includes the Nightcap art direction brief at docs/design/nightcap-art-direction.md, twelve text-based moodboards under docs/design/moodboards/, and the Host character bible at docs/design/the-host.md. AW-268 remains blocked until the AW-267 collaboration gates complete. No code, asset production, or engine change is authorized by this record.

Decision: D-074 AW-282 structured interaction loop approved
Rationale: Founder approved the AW-282 design and implementation plan after the discovery and scenario checkpoints recorded in docs/product/aw282-discovery-and-checkpoints.md. Build the game-agnostic Python interaction capability with authored question menus, deterministic evidence unlocks, per-beat count-specific question allowances, private selection and revision, rotated resolution order, public answer grouping, private asker feedback, and existing ContentEvent audience routing. Keep answer generation, contradiction metadata, and Leverage runtime behavior out of AW-282. Canonical implementation spec: docs/specs/0074-aw282-structured-interaction-loop.md; ADR: docs/decisions/0014-structured-interaction-resolution.md.

Decision: D-075 AW-287 Nightcap Leverage advantages and sabotages approved for implementation
Rationale: Founder approved moving Leverage from proposed design direction to implementation scope, sequenced before/alongside AW-283. The catalog, five-family taxonomy, and six-effect launch set (Deep Read, Follow the Thread, Sting Operation, Call Their Bluff, Rattle the Witness, Listen In) were already founder-directed in docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-18-nightcap-leverage-advantages-sabotages-design.md. This decision resolves that document's remaining listed unknowns: Leverage balances are public to all players; a sabotage's source is revealed to its target after the sabotaged interaction resolves, except Sting Operation's immediate-exposure payoff; Leverage carries between beats for the whole session, bounded by the existing bank-cap guardrail; per-effect visibility follows each effect's own documented behavior rather than one global public/private toggle; the team/co-op unknown is not applicable since v1 has no team/co-op configuration; the telemetry-signal unknown is deferred to post-rehearsal tuning. New task AW-287 tracks implementation (GitHub issue #250); AW-283's dependencies now include AW-287. Full record: docs/product/aw287-discovery-and-checkpoints.md.
Tags: nightcap, leverage, AW-287, AW-283, advantages, sabotages, economy

Decision: D-076 AW-287 Nightcap Leverage thin slice approved
Rationale: Founder approved the implemented AW-287 thin slice after reviewing an interactive replay of the founder-approved walkthrough scenario (docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-18-aw287-leverage-walkthrough.md). Implementation: generic engine/resources/ capability (models, targeting-eligibility resolver, event factories, session runtime, telemetry payload builders), the six launch-set effects configured on the real Couch Race arc (nightcap/couch-race.arc.json; Call Their Bluff replaced by Make Them Wait per ADR-0015), and nightcap/scripts/leverage_thin_slice_demo.py. Code-reviewed with all Critical/Important findings fixed, including a real game-agnosticism violation (Nightcap character names and a hardcoded arc path inside engine/, moved out to nightcap/scripts/ and the naming-contract test widened to catch this class of leak going forward). 700 engine tests pass; ruff check and format clean. Closes GitHub issue #250. AW-283 implementation may now proceed to consume this capability.
